---
title: "Fitabase User Activity Behavior & Product Usage Analysis"
author: "Chenxu Liu"
date: "2026-04-27"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# Introduction

This project aims to analyze smart device data to gain insight into how consumers are using the smart devices of Bellabeat, a high-tech manufacturer of health-focused products for women. The data is gathered from `https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/arashnic/fitbit/data`.

After cleaning the data using `MySQL`, several categories need to clarify for future discussion:

1. BMI category: 
$$
\mathbf{Thinnish}:\text{BMI} < 18.5~~~\mathbf{Normal}:18.5 \leq \text{BMI} < 25~~~\mathbf{Overweight}:25 \leq \text{BMI} < 30~~~\mathbf{Obesity}:\text{BMI} \geq 30
$$
2. User category:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\mathbf{Highly~Active~User}:\text{Average Steps}\geq 10000~~~\mathbf{Moderately~Active ~User}:7500 \leq \text{Average Steps} < 10000~~~\\
&\mathbf{Generally~Active~User}:5000 \leq \text{Average Steps} < 7500~~~\mathbf{Lightly~Active ~User}:\text{Average Steps < 5000}
\end{aligned}
$$

The analysis covers the data from 11 users who both record their activity and body information, and I gathered several key findings for company's future development:

1. $\mathbf{Significant~polarization~in~user~activity~levels}$: Highly active users (User 1, User 9, User 11) has an average over 11,000 daily steps, while lightly active users (User 2, User 5, User 6, User 10) has an average fewer than 5,000 steps. This gap shows extreme differences in exercise habits.

2. $\mathbf{Sedentary~behavior~dominates~users'~daily~activity}$: Most users spend a significant amount of their daily time in a sedentary state. Even highly active users have a sedentary time proportion exceeding 65%. It's crucial to set "reducing sedentary time and increasing exercise time" as the core direction for the product to inform users to maintain a healthier habit.

3. $\mathbf{Strong~correlation~between~exercise~intensity~and~health~status}$: Users with normal BMI have a significantly higher proportion of highly active exercise than overweight and obese users. It's evident that insufficient exercise is the main cause of weight gain.

4. $\mathbf{Improvement~in~data~quality}$: Some users have extreme outliers in their data, and it's necessary to improve device calibration and data validation mechanisms.

\newpage

# User Activity Level Segmentation and Overall Distribution

## 1. Activity Level Segmentation and Proportion

After analyzing user activity and activity time data, several useful figures are shown below based on the cleaned data:

```{r out.width="49%", fig.show="hold", fig.align="center", echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/User Average Step Distribution.png")
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/User Category Distribution.png")
```

```{r out.width="49%", fig.show="hold", fig.align="center", echo=FALSE, fig.cap="User Activity and Activity Time Summary"}
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/User Average Activity Distribution.png")
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/User Average Activity Time Distribution.png")
```

Based on average steps and exercise intensity data, the users are divided into 4 categories:

```{r warning = FALSE, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr)
activity <- data.frame(Activity_Level = c("Highly Active", "Moderately Active", 
                                          "Generally Active", "Lightly Active"),
                       Users = c("User 1, User 9, User 11", "User 3, User 8", 
                                 "User 4, User 7", "User 2, User 5, User 6, User 10"),
                       Average_Steps_Range = c("11,641-17,417", "7,943-9,800", 
                                               "5,785-6,637", "0-4,293"),
                       Proportion = c("27.27%", "18.18%", "18.18%", "36.36%"),
                       Feature = c("High proportion of very active exercise",
                                   "High proportion of moderately active exercise",
                                   "High proportion of lightly active exercise",
                                   "Almost no very active exercise"))
kable(activity, caption = "User Activity Level Segmentation", 
      col.names = c("Activity Level", "Users", "Average Step Range", "Proportion", "Feature"),
      align = "c")
```

## 2. Key insights:

- $\mathbf{Highly~active~users~are~ideal~users~of~the~product}$: User 11 is the top-performing user with an average of 17,417 daily steps and 51.87% very active share. User 1 and User 9 also have a very active share exceeding 30%. It's obvious that User 1, User 9, and User 11 are loyal users of the product.
- $\mathbf{Lightly~active~users~represent~the~dominance~of~the~user~base}$: Over 1/3 of users have an average fewer than 5,000 steps, with almost zero very active share (except User 11). These users are the core group to activate for future product development.
- $\mathbf{Positive~correlation~between~activity~level~and~exercise~intensity}$: Highly active users have a significantly higher share of very active exercise than lightly active users, while the opposite is true for the lightly active share. This finding demonstrates the rationality of the user segmentation.

# Deep Analysis of User Exercise Behavior and Time Allocation

## 1. Exercise Intensity Distribution (User Average Activity Distribution)

- $\mathbf{Highly~active~users}$: User 11 has a 51.87% very active share, while User 1 and User 9 have a very active share exceeding 30% and a relatively high moderately active share, with a lightly active share below 55%.
- $\mathbf{Lightly~active~users}$: User 2, User 5, and User 6 have a lightly active share exceeding 83% and a very active share below 15%, while User 10 has a relative balance between lightly active share and very active share.
- $\mathbf{Moderately/Generally~active~users}$: Both 2 types of users are dominated by lightly active exercise, while moderately active users (User 3 and User 8) have a larger moderately active share than generally active users (User 4 and User 7).

## 2. Exercise Time Allocation (User Average Activity Time Distribution)

- $\mathbf{Sedentary~behavior~dominates~users'~daily~activity}$: All users have a sedentary time share exceeding 65%. Among them, User 10 (95.10%), User 5 (92.36%), and User 2 (89.37%) have a sedentary share exceeding 85%, with almost no effective exercise time.
- $\mathbf{Reasonable~time~structure~for~highly~active~users}$: Highly active users have a higher active time share (Very active + Moderately active + Lightly active) exceeding 20% and a relatively low sedentary share (User 9 has a share with only 65.13%).
- $\mathbf{Mismatch~between~exercise~intensity~and~time}$: Some users have high step counts but a large sedentary share, which indicates their exercise is concentrated in a short time period with high intensity, while daily life remains dominated by sitting. It's obvious that these users should optimize their activity habits.

\newpage

## 3. Strong Correlation Between Steps and Calories Burned

The figures indicating the steps and calories burned are shown below by user category:

```{r out.width="49%", fig.show="hold", fig.align="center", echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/Highly Active User Data.png")
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/Moderately Active User Data.png")
```

```{r out.width="49%", fig.show="hold", fig.align="center", echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Walking Distance and Calories Consumption Summary (By User Category)"}
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/Generally Active User Data.png")
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/Lightly Active User Data.png")
```

Based on the figures, it's evident that the trends of `Walking Steps` and `Calories Consumption` have a similar pattern for all users except user 5 (due to some potential mistakes during calories record). This validates the basic rationality of the data and reflects that exercise intensity is the core factor of calorie consumption.

However, it's unreasonable that User 3 and User 8 have similar walking steps, while User 8 burned more calories than User 3. Besides, User 5 burns more calories than User 10, while User 5 has exercise data with 0 steps. Thus, the company should try to improve the mechanism of calculating the calorie consumption based on different indicators.

\newpage

# Correlation Analysis Between Health Metrics (BMI/Weight) and Exercise Intensity

## 1. Correspondence Between Health Segmentation and Activity Level

After analyzing health data for all users, several useful figures are shown below based on the cleaned data:

```{r out.width="32%", fig.show="hold", fig.align="center", echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Health Data Summary"}
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/Normal User Data.png")
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/Overweight User Data.png")
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/Obese User Data.png")
```

Based on health data for all users, the users are divided into 3 categories:

```{r warning = FALSE, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr)
health <- data.frame(Health_Level = c("Normal", "Overweight", "Obese"),
                     Users = c("User 1, User 3, User 4, User 9", 
                               "User 5, User 7, User 8, User 10, User 11", 
                               "User 2, User 6"),
                     BMI_Range = c("21.45-24.77", "25.03-29.55", "35.01-46.17"),
                     Corresponding_Activity_Level = c("Highly/Moderately Active", 
                                                      "Moderately/Generally Active",
                                                      "Generally/Lightly Active"),
                     Feature = c("Stable weight with low BMI",
                                 "Slight weight excess with slightly high BMI",
                                 "Severe weight excess with high BMI "))
kable(health, caption = "User Health Level Segmentation and Corresponding Activity Level", 
      col.names = c("Health Level", "Users", "BMI Range", "Corresponding Activity Level", "Feature"),
      align = "c")
```

## 2. Core Correlation Insights:

- $\mathbf{Strong~negative~correlation~between~BMI~and~activity~level}$: The higher the user's exercise intensity and very active share, the lower their BMI and the better their health status.
- $\mathbf{Lightly~active~users~needs~more~health~intervention~to~keep~healthy}$: Both obese users are in the lightly active level, and insufficient exercise is the core cause of weight excess, which requires a comprehensive exercise plan to inspire obese users to be healthy.
- $\mathbf{Better~health~status~for~highly~active~users}$: Among the three highly active users, two of them (User 1, User 9) are of normal weight, and only one (User 11) is overweight with a better health performance and proportion than other user levels.

\newpage

# Data Quality Analysis

## 1. Abnormal Data Identification

When analyzing the walking distance data, several users have data deviations that seem inaccurate when collecting the data:

```{r out.width="80%", fig.align="center", echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Inaccurate User Data"}
knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/25480/Desktop/Fitabase Data/Report Image/Inaccurate User Data.png")
```

Based on the figure above, it's obvious that:

- $\mathbf{User~14~has~the~largest~deviation}$: User 14 has a significant data difference of 6.730 km, which is a severely abnormal deviation. It is possibly caused by device failure, manual mistake, or incorrect wearing.
- $\mathbf{User~12~has~the~second~largest~deviation}$: User 12 has a difference of 3.220 km, which is about half of User 14's deviation. The reason might also be the same as User 14.
- Other users (User 13, 15, and 16) have deviations within 2 km, which are mild deviation values.

## 2. Product Optimization Directions

- $\mathbf{Data~validation~mechanism}$: Optimize data correction algorithms to reduce extreme outliers and improve data accuracy.
- $\mathbf{Anomaly~alert~function}$: Add data anomaly alerts for users to help avoid device-wearing issues and enhance user experience.
- $\mathbf{Data~cleaning~mechanism}$: Establish an anomaly data filtering mechanism in the back-end to improve report reliability and precision.

# Product Use and Operation Optimization Recommendations

## 1. Precision Operation Suggestions for Different User Levels

- $\mathbf{Highly~active~users}$: Build "exercise benchmarks", launch challenge tasks and badge systems to strengthen user stickiness; explore their exercise habits to provide references for other users.
- $\mathbf{Moderately/Generally~active~users}$: Set suitable goals to guide users to increase their very active exercise share and reduce sedentary time. Organize personalized exercise plans to gradually improve activity levels.
- $\mathbf{Lightly~active~users}$: Set check-in incentives and sedentary reminder functions to improve sedentary issues. Customize low-intensity entry exercise plans for obese/overweight users to activate their training passion.

## 2. Product Function Optimization

- $\mathbf{Sedentary~reminder~function}$: Strengthen sedentary reminders and push fragmented exercise suggestions to help users reduce daily sedentary time.
- $\mathbf{Health~data~report}$: Summarize exercise data and BMI/weight data to generate personalized health reports to provide a comprehensive exercise-health service for users.
- $\mathbf{Data~quality~improvement}$: Optimize device precision, add anomaly data validation function, and enhance data credibility.

## 3. Business Growth Directions

- $\mathbf{Lightly~active~user~activation}$: Over 1/3 of lightly active users are the core potential for product growth. Activating them through personalized operations can significantly improve overall activity and user satisfaction.
- $\mathbf{Health~data~connection}$: Based on the strong correlation between exercise and health, it's beneficial to constitute a core selling point of "exercise health management" to enhance product competitiveness in the market.
- $\mathbf{User~Segmentation~services}$: Achieve precision push based on user activity level and health indicators to improve personalized product experience.

# Final Summary

This analysis comprehensively illustrates the exercise behavior characteristics of users: 

- Highly active users have good exercise habits and excellent health status, which makes them the core users of the product.
- Lightly active users have insufficient exercise, severe sedentary behavior, and high health risks, which makes them the key activation target of the product. 

Meanwhile, the outliers in the data also reflect room for optimization in the product's data validation.

Through personalized user operations and product function optimization, we can effectively increase the product usage frequency of lightly active users, improve user health status, and achieve significant enhancement of product value and user health.
